section.title: Forgiveness for the Offender
section.sectionRationale: Church discipline had done its work: a man who had caused grief, likely by opposing Paul, had been punished by the majority and had repented. Now Paul turns the church from discipline to restoration. The punishment already imposed is enough; the danger now is not too little severity but too much. Paul urges them to forgive and comfort the offender, to reaffirm their love, lest excessive sorrow overwhelm him and lest Satan gain the advantage through an unforgiving church.
section.thesis: When a repentant offender has borne sufficient discipline, the church must move from punishment to forgiveness, comfort, and the reaffirming of love, forgiving as those who forgive in the presence of Christ, so that the sinner is restored and Satan gains no advantage over an unforgiving people.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the sufficient punishment of the offender and the call to forgive, comfort, and reaffirm love; and Paul's own forgiveness given in the presence of Christ, so that Satan should not outwit the church.

units.label: Sufficient punishment, now forgive
units.note: forgive and comfort, lest he be overwhelmed by sorrow

verses.text: Now if anyone has caused grief, he has not grieved me but all of you, to some degree, not to overstate it.
The punishment imposed on him by the majority is sufficient for him.
So instead, you ought to {{aphiemi|forgive}} and comfort him, so that he will not be overwhelmed by excessive sorrow.
Therefore I urge you to reaffirm your love for him.

units.label: Forgiven in the presence of Christ
units.note: so that Satan should not outwit us

verses.text: My purpose in writing you was to see if you would stand the test and be obedient in everything.
If you forgive anyone, I also forgive him. And if I have forgiven anything, I have forgiven it in the presence of Christ for your sake,
in order that Satan should not outwit us. For we are not unaware of his {{noema|schemes}}.

aphiemi.gloss: To send away, release, let go, forgive. The image is of releasing a debt or dismissing a charge, letting the offense go rather than holding it against the offender. It is the same verb Jesus uses when He teaches His disciples to pray for the forgiveness of debts. Here Paul urges the church to release the repentant man from the grip of his offense, joining forgiveness to comfort so that grace, not lingering condemnation, has the final word.

noema.gloss: A thought, design, scheme, or the mind that devises it. Paul uses the word for the calculated stratagems by which Satan seeks to harm the church. Here the devil's scheme is to turn discipline into destruction, to use an unforgiving spirit to crush a penitent believer and divide the body. Paul insists the church is not ignorant of such designs, so forgiveness becomes a weapon of spiritual warfare, guarding the flock from the enemy's cunning.

basic.context: A man in the church had caused serious grief, most likely by leading opposition against Paul. The church had disciplined him, and the discipline had worked: he had repented. Now Paul, far from demanding more severity, urges the opposite. The punishment already given is enough. It is time to forgive, comfort, and openly reaffirm love for him, so that he is not crushed by sorrow and so that Satan gains no foothold through an unforgiving church.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a case study in the goal of church discipline, which is always restoration, not destruction. Read verses 5 to 8 as the turn from punishment to forgiveness once repentance is real. Verse 11 reveals the spiritual stakes: unforgiveness is one of Satan's schemes. Paul models forgiveness done in the presence of Christ and for the sake of the whole body.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul urges the church to aphiemi, to forgive, to release the offender from his offense, the same verb Jesus uses for forgiving debts. And he warns that failing to do so plays into Satan's noema, his schemes or designs, the calculated stratagems the church must not be ignorant of.
basic.typology: The restoration of the repentant sinner reflects the God who does not deal with us according to our sins but removes our transgressions as far as the east is from the west. The church's forgiveness images the forgiveness of God in Christ, who welcomes back the penitent.
basic.christ: Paul forgives in the presence of Christ, so that Christ Himself is the pattern and the witness of the church's forgiveness. As Christ forgave freely and welcomes the repentant, so His people are to forgive and comfort. The whole ministry of reconciliation flows from the cross where forgiveness was purchased.
basic.god: God's heart is for the restoration of sinners, not their ruin. He disciplines, but His discipline aims at repentance and healing. The passage reflects a God who forgives the penitent and calls His people to mirror that mercy, guarding them against the enemy who would twist even good discipline into destruction.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 18 gives Jesus' pattern for church discipline aimed at winning the brother. Galatians 6 calls the spiritual to restore the fallen gently. Ephesians 4 says forgive as God in Christ forgave you. Luke 15 shows the father running to welcome the returning son.
basic.oneStory: The Bible tells of a God who restores the repentant. Discipline is real, but its goal is always to bring the sinner back, and its end is forgiveness, comfort, and renewed love. In forgiving the penitent, the church displays the mercy of God in Christ and stands guard against the schemes of the enemy who wants an unforgiving people.

context.literary: This short paragraph follows Paul's explanation of the tearful letter and continues the theme of grief and its resolution. The offender is never named, and Paul deliberately speaks with restraint and delicacy, unwilling to overstate the injury or to prolong shame. The section flows naturally into the wider theme of the ministry of reconciliation that dominates the letter.
context.historical: In the honor and shame culture of Corinth, public discipline carried real weight, and public restoration mattered just as much. The punishment imposed by the majority suggests a formal action of the gathered church. The offender is often thought to be someone who had openly challenged or insulted Paul during the painful visit, though the immoral man of 1 Corinthians 5 has also been suggested.
context.canonical: The passage is a key New Testament text on the aim of church discipline, complementing Matthew 18 and Galatians 6. It teaches that discipline without a path to restoration is incomplete, and that forgiveness is not optional sentiment but obedience and spiritual protection for the whole body.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To move the church from the completed act of discipline to the restoration of the repentant offender, urging them to forgive, comfort, and reaffirm their love, and to expose the failure to forgive as a scheme of Satan that endangers the whole community.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific case of the offender is described, but the principles are prescriptive: the goal of discipline is restoration, sufficient discipline should give way to forgiveness once there is repentance, forgiveness is to be active and public, and an unforgiving spirit opens the door to the enemy.

debates.question: Who is the offender Paul asks the church to forgive?

views.label: A man who had personally opposed or insulted Paul
views.case: The reference to grief caused to Paul and to the whole church, and the link to the painful visit and tearful letter, suggests someone who had publicly challenged Paul's authority, whom the church then disciplined.

views.label: The immoral man of 1 Corinthians 5
views.case: An older reading identifies him with the man under discipline for sexual sin in the previous letter, now repentant, though the emphasis on grief done to Paul fits a personal affront less obviously than a case of church purity.

debates.bearing: The offender's identity does not change the teaching; whoever he was, he had repented and borne sufficient discipline, and the church's duty was now forgiveness, comfort, and the reaffirming of love.

debates.question: How does refusing to forgive give Satan an advantage?

views.label: By crushing the penitent through excessive sorrow
views.case: Verse 7 warns that the man might be overwhelmed by excessive sorrow; unforgiveness could drive a repentant believer to despair and drive him from the church, which serves the enemy's aim.

views.label: By breeding bitterness and division in the body
views.case: An unforgiving spirit corrodes the whole community, fostering harshness, factions, and hypocrisy, so that Satan exploits the church's failure to reflect the mercy of Christ.

debates.bearing: Both dangers are real and related; Paul frames forgiveness as spiritual warfare, protecting both the individual sinner and the unity and witness of the whole church from the enemy's schemes.

study.originalLanguages: The pivot of the passage is aphiemi, to forgive, literally to send away or release, the same verb Jesus uses of releasing debts in the Lord's Prayer; the church is to let the offense go rather than hold it against the penitent, joining forgiveness to comfort. Set against this is Satan's noema, his scheme or design, a word for a calculated thought or stratagem. The devil's plan is to turn holy discipline into destruction, using an unforgiving spirit to overwhelm the repentant man and to fracture the body. Paul insists the church is not ignorant of such designs, so that forgiveness becomes an act of vigilant spiritual warfare.

typology.fulfillment: Mirrors the God who does not treat us as our sins deserve but removes our transgressions, and who welcomes the penitent home in Christ.

typology.type: Forgiveness in the presence of Christ
typology.fulfillment: The church's forgiveness images and flows from the forgiveness purchased at the cross, where reconciliation with God was accomplished.

study.christ: Paul forgives in the presence of Christ, making Christ both the pattern and the witness of the church's mercy. The forgiveness the church is to extend is the very forgiveness Christ secured at the cross and freely gives to the penitent. As Christ receives the repentant and restores the fallen, so His body is to forgive, comfort, and reaffirm love. The offender is welcomed back not on the basis of his own worth but in the mercy that flows from Christ, whose ministry of reconciliation the church continues.
study.god: God's heart is for the restoration of sinners. His discipline is never an end in itself but aims at repentance, healing, and renewed fellowship. He forgives the penitent fully and calls His people to mirror that mercy, refusing to let sorrow crush the one who has turned back. He is a God who guards His church against the enemy, and an unforgiving spirit grieves Him because it contradicts His own forgiving nature revealed in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Jesus' pattern for confronting sin in the church, aimed at winning the brother back.
Restore the one caught in sin gently, watching yourself lest you also be tempted.
Be kind and forgiving to one another, just as God in Christ forgave you.
The father runs to welcome the returning son, a picture of joyful restoration.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward the restoration of sinners to God and to one another. Discipline is real, but its goal is always to bring the wanderer back, and its proper end is forgiveness, comfort, and renewed love. When the church forgives the penitent in the presence of Christ, it displays the mercy of God purchased at the cross and stands guard against the enemy who longs for an unforgiving, divided people. Grace, not condemnation, is meant to have the final word.

quiz.q: What does Paul urge the church to do with the repentant offender?

options.text: Forgive him, comfort him, and reaffirm their love for him.
options.note: Verses 7 and 8 name all three: forgive, comfort, and reaffirm love.

options.text: Forgive him but keep him at arm's length.
options.note: Paul calls for active comfort and reaffirmed love, not distant tolerance.

options.text: Impose a stricter punishment to be sure he has learned.
options.note: Paul says the punishment already given is sufficient; more would be excessive.

options.text: Expel him permanently from the church.
options.note: The goal is restoration, not permanent exclusion.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say the punishment already given is sufficient?

options.text: Because the man has repented, and further sorrow could overwhelm him.
options.note: Verse 7 warns against his being overwhelmed by excessive sorrow.

options.text: Because discipline never accomplishes anything useful.
options.note: Discipline had done its work; Paul values it, but its aim is now met.

options.text: Because the offense was not really serious.
options.note: The grief was real; the point is that repentance has come, not that sin was trivial.

options.text: Because Paul no longer cared about the matter.
options.note: Paul cares deeply, which is why he seeks the man's restoration.

quiz.q: In whose presence does Paul say he forgives?

options.text: In the presence of Christ, for the sake of the church.
options.note: Verse 10 grounds forgiveness before Christ and for the body's good.

options.text: In the presence of the church elders only.
options.note: The elders may witness it, but Paul names Christ Himself as the witness.

options.text: In private, where no one could see.
options.note: Paul's forgiveness is open and for the church's sake, not hidden.

options.text: In the presence of the offender alone.
options.note: Paul forgives before Christ and for the sake of all, not merely privately to the man.

quiz.q: Why is forgiving the offender described as spiritual warfare?

options.text: So that Satan should not outwit the church, whose schemes they know.
options.note: Verse 11 frames forgiveness as guarding against Satan's designs.

options.text: Because forgiveness earns a reward in the battle against evil.
options.note: Forgiveness protects the church; it is not a merit that earns reward.

options.text: Because Satan cannot tempt a forgiving person at all.
options.note: Forgiveness closes one door to the enemy, but believers still face temptation.

options.text: Because unforgiveness has no spiritual consequences.
options.note: Paul says the opposite: unforgiveness gives Satan an advantage.

questions: The goal of discipline is restoration, not destruction. Is there someone you have disciplined or corrected whom God may now be calling you to forgive, comfort, and welcome back?
Paul warns against a penitent being overwhelmed by excessive sorrow. Do you tend to withhold comfort from those who have repented, and how might you actively reaffirm your love for them?
Paul forgave in the presence of Christ. How does remembering that Christ witnesses and grounds your forgiveness change the way you forgive those who have wronged you?
Unforgiveness is named as one of Satan's schemes. Where might a refusal to forgive be giving the enemy an advantage in your life or your church?
Reaffirming love means making forgiveness visible, not merely internal. What concrete step could you take this week to restore a relationship damaged by sin?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads he has not grieved me but all of you, to some degree, not to overstate it; the original uses a dash before to some degree, replaced here with a comma. Paul softens the statement so as not to exaggerate the injury.
Forgive renders aphiemi, to release or let go, the same verb used for forgiving debts; it pictures releasing the offender from the hold of his offense.
Schemes renders noema, meaning thoughts, designs, or stratagems; Paul warns that the church is not ignorant of Satan's calculated designs against it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 2:5-11. Dash substitution: 2:5 the em dash before to some degree was replaced with a comma, so it reads but all of you, to some degree, not to overstate it. Sermon set to none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
